Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed declarative clause with possessive/divine noun phrase and stative verb; used as an evaluative proverb-like idiom.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Good character brings honour, respect, and social value.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ọmọ nẹ r’ẹvwẹ ọma, ẹyẹ ọghọ na.
Usage Contexts: family advice; moral instruction; school; public praise; community speech
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Rooted in Urhobo communal ethics where reputation, kinship honour, and respectful conduct are publicly valued.
First Example Sentence: Ẹvwẹ r’óghene vwo ọghọ, ọvo nẹ me cha kẹ ọmọ na.
Second Example Sentence: Ọmọ nẹ r’ẹvwẹ ọma, ẹvwẹ r’óghene vwo ọghọ n’ẹruvwu.
Third Example Sentence: Avware vwọ ẹvwẹ r’óghene vwo ọghọ, rọ omamọ na vwẹ r’ẹkọ.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative fixed clause with abstract noun subject and locative complement; proverbial metaphor.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Honour cannot be hidden; true worth eventually becomes known.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ọmọ nẹ ọma, udọn rẹ ma gbevwi.
Usage Contexts: praise; leadership; community judgment; workplace recognition; encouragement
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Connected to village life where public reputation spreads through markets, age grades, and family networks.
First Example Sentence: Rọ vwẹ ruru ọghwẹ rẹ; ọghọ ma dje n’ẹkpẹ.
Second Example Sentence: Ọghọ ma dje n’ẹkpẹ, ọmọ nẹ r’ọrụ ọma a kpe rẹ.
Third Example Sentence: Ufuoma yọ erhiẹrẹvwe, k’ọghọ ma dje n’ẹkpẹ.

Idiom (Urhobo): Ufuoma nọ khọre, ọghwẹ nọ rọvie.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Balanced parallel clause; nominal subject plus relative/stative predicates; aphoristic contrast.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Peace may look quiet, but it often protects people from trouble.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ufuoma gbe ẹruvwu na rọ ọfọ.
Usage Contexts: conflict resolution; family meetings; politics; workplace mediation; counselling
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects Urhobo preference for settlement, elder mediation, and preventing quarrels from damaging kinship ties.
First Example Sentence: Mẹ ta ufuoma nọ khọre, ọghwẹ nọ rọvie, k’ẹjoviẹn ma r’ọvwọ.
Second Example Sentence: Kẹ eghwa, ufuoma nọ khọre, ọghwẹ nọ rọvie na wẹ.
Third Example Sentence: Ọnana ma jẹ oro; ufuoma nọ khọre, ọghwẹ nọ rọvie.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative declarative clause with animate subject and object complement; metaphorical fixed saying.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: An elder does not act without thought; experience should guide judgment.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ọmọ r’ọmẹn ma ru iṣẹ kẹ ọghwẹ.
Usage Contexts: elders’ counsel; family correction; dispute settlement; leadership; mentorship
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Anchored in the Urhobo social respect for elders as custodians of memory, precedent, and communal wisdom.
First Example Sentence: Ẹkpako ma roro ovwan, dọvwẹ rẹ ọtẹ r’ọmẹn na.
Second Example Sentence: Ọghwẹ na ma ye mi vẹrẹ, ẹkpako ma roro ovwan.
Third Example Sentence: Nẹ ọvie ta, ẹkpako ma roro ovwan, avware de.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative clause with abstract noun subject and locative/instrumental complement; metaphor of inner truth.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: The truth cannot be completely erased from the heart or conscience.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Oro r’ọghwẹ ma se kẹ udọn ọmọ.
Usage Contexts: justice; apology; moral reflection; religion; courtroom-style discussion
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Echoes Urhobo moral discourse on conscience, oath-taking, and the belief that wrongdoing remains known to the inner person.
First Example Sentence: Ọ ta vwọ aro, ṣedẹ ẹruvwu ma gberhe kẹ ẹvwẹ.
Second Example Sentence: Ẹruvwu ma gberhe kẹ ẹvwẹ, ọghwẹ na rẹ udọn rẹ.
Third Example Sentence: K’ẹjọ na, ẹruvwu ma gberhe kẹ ẹvwẹ, rọ ta ọghwẹ.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative metaphorical clause with concrete noun subject and locative complement; food imagery.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A small problem does not disappear just because it is mixed into a bigger matter.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ẹghwẹ kpọ na ma se n’oro wọhọ.
Usage Contexts: family finance; politics; troubleshooting; medical explanation; workplace risk
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Uses food imagery familiar in Delta communities, where communal meals make small ingredients visible within a larger pot.
First Example Sentence: Má fiẹ ọghwẹ na; ovwian ma guọ kẹ ukodo.
Second Example Sentence: Ovwian ma guọ kẹ ukodo, rọ rẹ ọghwẹ kpọ na kẹ emu.
Third Example Sentence: N’ọrụ na, ovwian ma guọ kẹ ukodo; rọ ru ẹrhọ.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Relative-clause idiom with abstract noun head and body-part object; metaphorical construction.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A matter is too serious to ignore; it demands attention.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Oro na ga, rọ gba ka rẹ.
Usage Contexts: warnings; politics; family decisions; health matters; business urgency
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Body-part imagery is common in Urhobo rhetoric, using the ear to symbolize attention, listening, and social responsibility.
First Example Sentence: Oro wọhọ nọ kpẹ ẹhọ, ọkara ma re kẹ urhukpe.
Second Example Sentence: Nẹ avware gbọ vwọ, oro wọhọ nọ kpẹ ẹhọ na.
Third Example Sentence: Ẹjọ na ma jẹ ẹgbọ; oro wọhọ nọ kpẹ ẹhọ.

Idiom (Urhobo): Ẹvwẹ r’ovie ma yẹ kẹ ẹfuru.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative declarative clause with genitive noun phrase and locative complement; status metaphor.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A person of dignity should not behave carelessly or disgracefully.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ọmọ r’ọghọ yẹ rọ ru avwanre ọma.
Usage Contexts: leadership; public conduct; chieftaincy; workplace ethics; formal advice
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Draws on Urhobo ideas of title, kingship, and the public burden of honour carried by leaders and respected families.
First Example Sentence: Ẹvwẹ r’ovie ma yẹ kẹ ẹfuru, dọvwẹ rẹ orere na.
Second Example Sentence: Nẹ ọvie r’ẹruvwu, ẹvwẹ r’ovie ma yẹ kẹ ẹfuru.
Third Example Sentence: Ọ ta vwọ ọkpọ, ṣedẹ ẹvwẹ r’ovie ma yẹ kẹ ẹfuru.

Idiom (Urhobo): Ukoko nọ gba, ọmẹ ma ro.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Conditional-like parallel proverb with noun subject clauses; cause-and-effect metaphor.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: When there is pressure or danger, comfort disappears.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Nẹ ẹghwẹ wọhọ se, ufuoma ma ku.
Usage Contexts: hardship; economic stress; family pressure; workplace deadlines; social commentary
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Links domestic water and vessel imagery to everyday survival, especially in households where scarcity changes behaviour.
First Example Sentence: Ukoko nọ gba, ọmẹ ma ro; ọghwẹ na jẹ kẹ avware.
Second Example Sentence: N’ẹghwẹ r’ẹkpẹ, ukoko nọ gba, ọmẹ ma ro.
Third Example Sentence: Má kpọ ara rẹ; ukoko nọ gba, ọmẹ ma ro, ṣedẹ avware cha.

Idiom (Urhobo): Ọvwọ nọ r’ẹhọ, ọ gbọ oro.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Relative-clause subject plus main clause; body-part possession idiom indicating receptiveness.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A person who listens carefully understands what is being said.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Ọmọ nẹ riẹ ẹhọ ọma, ọ rọ mẹ udọn oro.
Usage Contexts: teaching; parenting; meetings; language learning; advice
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects oral-learning traditions where listening well is central to apprenticeship, storytelling, and elder-child instruction.
First Example Sentence: Ọvwọ nọ r’ẹhọ, ọ gbọ oro; rọ riẹ ẹhọ rẹ kẹ ẹkpako.
Second Example Sentence: Nẹ omote na riẹ ẹhọ, ọvwọ nọ r’ẹhọ, ọ gbọ oro.
Third Example Sentence: Ọkpọ na ma wọhọ; ọvwọ nọ r’ẹhọ, ọ gbọ oro.
